title: Squire for Hire - a micro, tile-laying, score-chasing, solo puzzle game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_3MWGJ7MUTA

Daniel (@dungeondive) looks at Squire for Hire, a charming micro tile-laying game by John Merchant for one to two players, about 15 minutes a game. You play a squire organising the hero's loot bag: you cover items by overlapping cards (each placement must fully cover an item, never partially), chasing points for weapons, armour, magic and valuables while minimising exposed "junk," and aiming for 25+ in solo play. Each of the four squires scores differently — Uru the raccoon, for instance, treats food-junk as zero and gets bonus magic — which supplies the variety.

A double-sided adventure deck drives play: one side poses quests, dungeons and encounters (pay a cost from your bag), the other side is the loot you slot in, with adjacency of matching items earning bonus points. He demonstrates a full game, hunting clever placements that make a single tile satisfy two scoring connections at once.

The rules are a touch cramped (he recommends a how-to-play video), but it's quick, portable and genuinely fun — he played three back-to-back at a bar. He also picked up the second core set (Mystic Runes) and a Squire pack adding eighteen more squires for extra scoring variety.

----

Does a light, score-chasing tile-laying puzzle scratch a different itch than a full dungeon crawl for you — and do you prefer it solo or two-player?
